articles.title: Croatia's Growth Story Rings Hollow for Protesters as Inflation Bites Hardest in the Eurozone
articles.excerpt: Thousands of Croatians have taken to the streets of Zagreb demanding higher wages, pensions, and relief from the eurozone's steepest inflation, now running at 4.89% as of March 2026. The protests expose a widening gap between Croatia's solid macroeconomic indicators and the daily financial pressures felt by ordinary citizens. The unrest poses a direct political challenge to Prime Minister Andrej Plenković's government, which points to low unemployment and price controls as evidence of sound management.
articles.body:
Marching Against the Cost of Living

The banners filling Zagreb's central squares this spring carry a blunt message: "Stop the tax of poverty." Union leaders, pensioners, and healthcare workers have joined a broad coalition demanding that the minimum wage rise to €1,100 net per month, that average wages reach €2,200 net, and that pensions be lifted to an average of €1,100. Those figures stand sharply above current levels, where the minimum wage sits at roughly €800 net, average wages at approximately €1,500 net, and average pensions at around €700 per month.

The timing is driven by data that is difficult to dispute. Croatia recorded the eurozone's highest inflation rate in March 2026, at 4.89%, according to national statistical sources. Food expenditure accounts for 26.7% of household budgets in Croatia, compared with 16.5% in neighbouring Slovenia and just 12.5% in Germany. For retirees living on fixed pensions, and for the young workers who might otherwise anchor the country's future workforce, those numbers translate directly into difficult daily choices.

Mladen Novosel, a prominent union leader, argued that the protesters' demands are "not unreasonable," given Croatia's position in the EU wage league table. Independent analysis places Croatia 19th among EU member states on wages despite the country recording the sixth-highest food prices in the bloc, a disparity that lends credibility to the unions' case. Corruption and the persistent grey economy, critics add, compound these pressures by shrinking the tax base and distorting labour markets.

Government Points to Stability, Critics See Selective Vision

The Plenković administration has not been without counterarguments. Officials highlight a suite of stabilising measures introduced over the past two years: price controls on gasoline, utilities, and a basket of basic goods; an unemployment rate below 5%; balanced budgets; and improved sovereign credit ratings that reflect growing international confidence in Croatia's fiscal management. Inflation, the government notes, has been declining since late 2024, suggesting that the worst of the post-euro-adoption price spike may have passed.

Croatia joined the eurozone in January 2023, and officials argue that membership has brought long-term structural benefits, including lower borrowing costs and stronger absorption of EU cohesion funds, which are expected to support the projected 2.9% GDP growth in 2026. The European Commission's figures also show the deficit holding at 2.8% of GDP and debt-to-GDP at a manageable 56%, metrics that would be the envy of several larger eurozone members.

Yet independent assessments from the OECD and the Bertelsmann Transformation Index suggest that macroeconomic progress has not translated evenly into household welfare. Price controls have provided partial relief but have not closed the gap with eurozone peers, and inflation perceptions among consumers remain elevated even as headline figures ease. The European Commission's own growth forecast acknowledges that consumption-led expansion depends on wages rising in real terms — a condition that protesters argue has not yet been met.

A Demographic Time Bomb and the EU's Periphery Problem

Beyond the immediate political friction, the protests illuminate a longer-term structural risk. Croatia has experienced significant emigration over the past decade, particularly among younger, skilled workers drawn to higher wages in Germany, Austria, and Ireland. If real incomes remain stagnant relative to the cost of living, union leaders warn that the outflow will accelerate, deepening labour shortages in sectors such as healthcare and construction that are already operating under strain. Novosel and other union officials have threatened escalating action through 2028 if demands go unmet, signalling that the current unrest may be the opening act of a prolonged confrontation.

For the European Union, Croatia's situation is a case study in the tensions inherent in eurozone expansion. Peripheral members that adopt the euro surrender monetary policy flexibility at the very moment when their wage and price structures are still converging toward core-country levels. Croatia's 4.89% March inflation rate, the highest in the currency bloc, underscores that convergence can be painful and uneven, raising questions about whether cohesion fund allocation adequately compensates for the adjustment costs borne by lower-income member states.

The government's next move is likely to involve a combination of incremental wage negotiations through social dialogue and further targeted subsidies ahead of any formal review of its price control regime. With the 2027 EU budget cycle under negotiation and forecasts projecting inflation falling to 2.2% only by that year, the window for political manoeuvre is narrow. If growth at 2.9% delivers wage gains that outpace prices over the next twelve to eighteen months, the government may yet defuse the protests. If it does not, Croatia's streets are likely to grow louder, and Brussels will face mounting pressure to reassess how the bloc supports its newest and most economically stretched eurozone members.
articles.keywords: Croatia, Zagreb protests, inflation, eurozone, cost of living, wages, pensions, Andrej Plenković, GDP growth, labour unions, emigration, EU cohesion funds, price controls, European Commission
articles.sources: Croatian Bureau of Statistics, March 2026 inflation data; European Commission economic forecast, 2026; OECD wage and living standards assessments; Bertelsmann Transformation Index Croatia report; Eurostat household expenditure data; National Bank of Croatia fiscal indicators; Union statements by Mladen Novosel, Zagreb protests 2026

articles.title: Romania's Coalition Collapse Puts Billions in EU Funds and Economic Stability at Risk
articles.excerpt: Romania's governing coalition fractured on April 20, 2026, when the Social Democratic Party withdrew over austerity measures, leaving Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan's government exposed to a coordinated no-confidence vote. The political rupture threatens access to more than 30 billion euros in EU recovery funds, while state-owned companies continue to bleed nearly 2.7 billion euros in losses. For ordinary Romanians, the stakes range from pension security to infrastructure projects financed by Brussels.
articles.body:
Coalition Falls Apart Over Austerity Measures

The immediate trigger for Romania's latest political crisis was the Social Democratic Party's formal withdrawal from the four-party governing coalition on April 20, 2026. The PSD, one of the country's largest parties by parliamentary seat count, said it could no longer support a reform package that includes raising value-added tax from 19 to 21 percent, increasing the dividend tax to 16 percent, cutting public sector personnel spending by 10 percent, and restructuring social outlays. Party leaders demanded Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan's resignation, arguing the reforms were pushing the burden of fiscal adjustment onto workers and pensioners.

Bolojan, backed by the National Liberal Party and the reformist Save Romania Union, rejected that framing. He argued that the PSD was "shirking responsibility" on decisions that are fiscally unavoidable, pointing to persistent budget deficits and conditions attached to Romania's National Recovery and Resilience Plan. The prime minister also signalled that any future governing arrangement would exclude the PSD entirely, regardless of how the no-confidence motion resolves.

The PSD has since aligned with the far-right Alliance for Romanian Unity to coordinate a joint no-confidence vote. Together, the two parties hold approximately 220 parliamentary seats, according to current legislative tallies. The motion requires 233 votes to succeed, leaving its sponsors 13 seats short at present. Coalition officials and analysts are watching smaller parliamentary groups closely to see whether the opposition can close that gap.

State Companies Deepen the Fiscal Wound

Compounding the political turbulence is a separate but related crisis in Romania's state-owned enterprise sector. Twenty-two government-controlled companies reported combined losses of approximately 2.7 billion euros, described by officials as a historic low point in the sector's financial performance. Translated into domestic currency, the figure amounts to roughly 14 billion lei, a sum that analysts said places additional strain on a budget already under pressure from the European Commission.

Deputy Prime Minister Oana Gheorghiu put forward a pilot recovery memorandum designed to restructure the worst-performing firms and halt further losses. The plan was due to go before the cabinet for approval in the days following April 17, 2026, but its progress has been complicated by the coalition's disintegration. Gheorghiu framed the initiative as an urgent fiscal necessity, tying it directly to Romania's broader obligations under the PNRR. Government officials said the restructuring also reflects conditions that Brussels has set for disbursing recovery funds.

The PSD opposed the reorganization plan as part of what it described as a sweeping and socially damaging austerity agenda. Former infrastructure minister Sorin Grindeanu also publicly criticized Gheorghiu's appointment, pointing to her past statements about the state enterprise sector. The opposition's resistance has slowed legislative momentum around the reforms at precisely the moment when fiscal consolidation is most urgent, according to government officials.

EU Funds, Elections, and a Narrowing Window

The stakes extend well beyond Bucharest's parliamentary arithmetic. Romania's PNRR allocation exceeds 30 billion euros, covering infrastructure upgrades, digital transformation, and climate investment. Key disbursement milestones are tied to reform implementation, and officials have warned that the window for meeting those conditions closes in August 2026. A prolonged government crisis, or the formation of a replacement administration with a different policy orientation, could jeopardize progress on those targets and leave funding unspent or subject to clawback.

Romania's National Bank had previously flagged the drag that underperforming state-owned companies exert on public finances, a warning that predates the current political standoff. With the enterprise losses now confirmed at 2.7 billion euros and the restructuring plan stalled, the risk is that fiscal slippage deepens regardless of which political configuration emerges from the no-confidence process. Eurostat data on Romania's deficit trajectory reinforces the government's argument that inaction carries a higher long-term cost than the short-term pain of adjustment.

For Romanian citizens, the consequences are tangible. Delays in PNRR-funded infrastructure projects would slow regional development and relief from inflation-era economic pressures. Uncertainty over the pension and public salary framework unsettles households already navigating a high cost of living. On the broader European stage, prolonged instability in a country that anchors NATO's eastern flank and borders Ukraine introduces risks that Brussels, Berlin, and Warsaw are all monitoring. The coming weeks, centered on the no-confidence vote and the cabinet's handling of the enterprise memorandum, will determine whether Romania can stabilize its finances before the EU's summer deadlines arrive.
articles.keywords: Romania, coalition crisis, no-confidence vote, Ilie Bolojan, PSD, AUR, PNRR, EU funds, austerity, state-owned enterprises, Romanian politics, European Union, fiscal reform, VAT increase, budget deficit
articles.sources: Romanian Government coalition statements, April 2026; PSD withdrawal announcement, April 20, 2026; PNRR progress reporting, European Commission; National Bank of Romania warnings on state-owned enterprise finances; Romanian parliamentary seat distribution data; Deputy PM Oana Gheorghiu recovery memorandum briefing, April 2026

articles.title: Slovenia Faces Twin Pressures of Economic Pessimism and Political Deadlock
articles.excerpt: Slovenia's economic sentiment has fallen to its lowest point since mid-2023, driven by rising inflation expectations and consumer anxiety linked to the Middle East conflict. The country simultaneously lacks a permanent government following inconclusive elections in March, leaving critical fiscal and structural reforms in limbo. Together, these developments pose a meaningful challenge for a small, export-dependent economy navigating an increasingly volatile European landscape.
articles.body:
War-Driven Uncertainty Drags Down Economic Mood

Slovenia's economic sentiment indicator fell below its long-term average in April, registering its weakest reading since the middle of 2023. The primary driver, according to data from the country's Institute of Macroeconomic Analysis and Development, known as UMAR or IMAD, is the ripple effect of the Middle East conflict on energy prices and global trade confidence. Brent crude oil surged by 45.5 percent in March, amplifying inflationary pressures across the eurozone and hitting import-dependent economies particularly hard.

Consumer confidence dropped by approximately 10 percentage points, the steepest single decline since 2022, reflecting widespread anxiety over the cost of living. Slovenia's harmonised index of consumer prices, aligned with Eurostat methodology, registered inflation of 2.4 percent in March. While that figure remains below recent highs, Banka Slovenije has warned that upward pressure from wages and energy costs has not fully dissipated. For ordinary households, the combination of higher utility bills and food prices is eroding real wage gains achieved over the past two years, even as nominal pay rose in the public and private sectors.

The government has pointed to energy price controls and continued inflows of EU structural funds as stabilising forces, arguing that growth in the first quarter remained on track. The International Monetary Fund, in its latest projections, assumed the conflict would fade as a macroeconomic variable by mid-2026, a scenario the caretaker administration cited in defence of its measured response. Business groups and opposition parties, however, argued this framing understated immediate export risks, particularly to Germany, Slovenia's largest trading partner, where industrial output has been contracting for several consecutive quarters.

Post-Election Limbo Deepens Reform Paralysis

Compounding the economic headwinds is a political vacuum that has persisted since parliamentary elections on March 22. No party secured a majority capable of forming a stable coalition, leaving a caretaker administration in place with limited authority to initiate legislation, approve new budgetary measures, or advance structural reforms. Slovenia's political stability index, derived from World Bank governance indicators, has declined to 0.72, down from 0.86 in 2023, reflecting the deterioration in governance confidence that regional analysts at bne IntelliNews have described as a period of stalled reform momentum.

The practical consequences are tangible. Legislative proposals addressing pension system sustainability, housing affordability, and fiscal consolidation have been suspended in parliamentary committee. Trade unions have publicly accused the caretaker government of inaction, particularly in the context of inflation-driven wage negotiations in the broader services sector. The government effectiveness score of 1.09, while still relatively high in regional terms, has not translated into visible policy output during the transition period, critics said.

GDP growth forecasts from IMAD project a modest 1.0 percent expansion for 2025, rising to 2.4 percent in 2026 and 2.6 percent by 2027, contingent on both political normalisation and a stabilisation of the external environment. Those projections, however, were constructed against a backdrop of eurozone growth now forecast at between 0.9 and 1.1 percent by the ECB and IMF respectively, leaving limited room for upside surprises should coalition talks drag into the summer.

Outlook: Narrow Window Before Pressures Compound

The convergence of external economic shocks and internal political uncertainty creates a narrowing window for Slovenia to restore both growth momentum and institutional confidence. Coalition negotiations are continuing, but no timeline for a new government has been publicly confirmed. Each additional week of caretaker status increases the risk that pending EU co-funded investment projects face administrative delays, a concern that carries weight given Slovenia's historically strong record of cohesion fund absorption, which has supported infrastructure and research spending over the past decade.

On the economic side, the key near-term variables are energy price trajectories and the pace of German industrial recovery. Slovenia exports a significant share of its manufactured goods, including automotive components and pharmaceuticals, to central European and German supply chains. A prolonged slowdown in German demand, already reflected in weakening Slovenian industrial output data, would test the resilience of an economy where export revenues account for a disproportionately large share of gross domestic product relative to the country's size.

For EU policymakers, Slovenia's situation offers a microcosm of the broader challenge facing smaller member states: the interaction between geopolitical volatility, energy dependency, and domestic political fragility. If a new government is formed before the summer recess and moves swiftly on fiscal stabilisation measures, analysts said the 2026 growth projection of around 2.4 percent remains achievable. Absent that, the combination of persistently high inflation expectations, weakening consumer sentiment, and unrealised structural reforms risks locking Slovenia into a more prolonged period of underperformance relative to its central European peers.
articles.keywords: Slovenia, economic sentiment, political vacuum, inflation, Middle East conflict, consumer confidence, GDP growth, eurozone, caretaker government, coalition talks, EU funds, export economy, energy prices, HICP, political stability
articles.sources: UMAR/IMAD macroeconomic analysis and development reports; Banka Slovenije monetary and financial stability assessments; Eurostat harmonised index of consumer prices, March data; IMF World Economic Outlook projections; ECB eurozone growth forecasts; World Bank governance indicators; bne IntelliNews Slovenia political analysis

articles.title: Bulgaria's Budget Crisis Shakes Eurozone Hopes as Mass Protests Force Government Retreat
articles.excerpt: Bulgaria's government has withdrawn its controversial 2026 draft budget following the largest street protests the country has seen in years, plunging the country's fiscal planning into uncertainty just as it prepares for euro adoption. Tens of thousands of demonstrators took to the streets demanding transparency and an end to what they described as oligarchic corruption embedded in public spending. The reversal raises fresh questions about whether Sofia can deliver the political stability and fiscal credibility required to join the eurozone on schedule.
articles.body:
Record Protests Bring Down a Budget Before It Begins

In scenes that drew comparisons to Bulgaria's earlier waves of anti-corruption unrest, an estimated 50,000 to 70,000 protesters gathered across the country to oppose the government's proposed 2026 budget, forcing a dramatic policy reversal within days. Demonstrators chanted "Mafia out!" and "Don't feed the pig," directing their anger at what they described as a spending plan designed to benefit a narrow political and business elite at the expense of ordinary citizens.

The budget had proposed significant increases in social security contributions and a doubling of the dividend tax, alongside total public expenditure reaching 46 percent of gross domestic product — a record level financed through a combination of debt and new taxation. Critics, including trade unions and opposition parties, argued the plan froze benefits for vulnerable households while rewarding well-connected interests through opaque line items. The scale of public anger was sufficient to compel the government to announce a withdrawal and revision of the draft, promising to scrap the tax hikes, cut what officials acknowledged was extravagant spending, and commit to keeping public debt below 40 percent of GDP without raising the overall tax burden through 2028.

The episode marks the latest in a prolonged series of political disruptions in Bulgaria, which has now held eight general elections in four years. That chronic instability has left institutions weakened and public trust in government at persistently low levels, creating fertile ground for the kind of mass mobilisation witnessed over recent days.

Eurozone Accession Caught in the Crossfire

The political turmoil arrives at a particularly sensitive moment. Bulgaria is officially targeting 2026 for adoption of the euro, a milestone that requires not only meeting the Maastricht fiscal criteria but also demonstrating a degree of institutional reliability that snap elections and budget collapses tend to undermine. The government had framed the original budget as a necessary exercise in fiscal prudence aligned with eurozone convergence requirements, arguing that difficult spending decisions today would yield long-term stability.

That argument has found little traction on the streets. A significant portion of protesters tied their opposition to broader fears about euro adoption itself, expressing concern that currency changeover would accelerate inflation and erode purchasing power — anxieties that analysts note are not uncommon in accession economies but which are amplified here by a pattern of deficits, institutional mistrust, and the perception that the euro project serves elite rather than popular interests. While no Eurostat or National Bank data in current circulation directly confirms a specific inflation shock scenario, Bulgaria's fiscal volatility and record of incomplete structural reforms give credibility to concerns about its readiness.

For the European Union more broadly, the situation carries implications beyond one member state's monetary timeline. Bulgaria remains a significant recipient of EU structural and cohesion funds, and persistent political instability has historically correlated with slower absorption rates. Any further delay in fiscal consolidation or reform implementation could strain both Sofia's credibility in Brussels and the EU's wider ability to demonstrate that enlargement and deepening integration can proceed in tandem.

What Comes Next: Revised Budget, Uncertain Mandate

The government now faces the difficult task of producing a revised budget that satisfies both domestic political pressure and the external requirements of eurozone accession — two sets of demands that do not always point in the same direction. Officials have stated that the revised plan will eliminate the contested tax increases and reduce spending, while maintaining a commitment to debt discipline. Whether that revised document can command sufficient parliamentary support in a fragmented legislature remains far from clear.

The protests are unlikely to dissipate quickly. Trade unions and civil society organisations have signalled that they regard the budget withdrawal as a first step rather than a resolution, continuing to demand greater transparency in public procurement and clearer accountability mechanisms for how EU funds are allocated and spent. The persistence of chants linking the budget to systemic corruption suggests that even a technically improved fiscal plan may struggle to restore public confidence in the near term.

For Bulgaria's eurozone ambitions, the coming weeks represent a critical test. European partners and institutions will be watching closely to see whether Sofia can present a credible, revised fiscal framework before the end of the year, and whether the government retains the political cohesion to pass it. A failure to do so would likely push euro adoption beyond 2026, with knock-on consequences for investor confidence, EU fund disbursement schedules, and Bulgaria's standing within the bloc at a time when the country can ill afford fresh uncertainty.

articles.title: Greece Navigates a Paradox of Growth and Discontent as Mitsotakis Faces Eroding Mandate
articles.excerpt: Greece's economy is outpacing much of the eurozone, yet the government of Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis is losing political ground as voters grow weary of scandals, rising rents, and uneven recovery. With Nea Dimokratia's polling lead shrinking significantly and the opposition fragmented, the country faces a period of political uncertainty that could complicate its economic trajectory. The tension between creditable macroeconomic data and street-level discontent is now defining Greek politics.
articles.body:
A Shrinking Lead in a Fragmented Landscape

Nea Dimokratia, the ruling centre-right party, remains the dominant force in Greek politics but is no longer the commanding presence it once was. Opinion surveys now place the party at around 25 to 31 percent support, down from roughly 40 percent at its peak, a decline that reflects voter fatigue after years in power and the cumulative weight of several damaging controversies.

The opposition, rather than coalescing into a credible alternative, has splintered across ideological lines. The centre-left PASOK trails below 15 percent, the once-dominant SYRIZA has collapsed to between 5 and 7 percent, while Hellenic Solution, a nationalist party, has settled at 10 to 12 percent. A further 7 to 10 percent of voters are distributed among smaller movements, including a new civic initiative linked to Magda Karystianou, a campaigner who emerged from the aftermath of the 2023 Tempi rail disaster and whose movement draws around 31.8 percent sympathy among those seeking systemic change, according to recent polling data.

This fragmentation raises a practical and structural concern: under Greece's electoral system, no single party currently appears positioned to govern alone, making coalition arithmetic increasingly complex. Analysts warn that a hung parliament would test institutions still rebuilding their credibility following the economic crisis of the previous decade.

Scandals and the Cost of Living Deepen Public Frustration

The political headwinds facing Mitsotakis are not merely the product of time in office. Two episodes in particular have embedded themselves in public consciousness. The Predator spyware affair, in which Greek journalists and politicians were reportedly surveilled using commercial surveillance software, raised serious rule-of-law questions that opposition parties have repeatedly pressed. The Tempi rail crash of February 2023, which killed 57 people and exposed systemic failures in railway management and state oversight, continues to define perceptions of governmental accountability.

Beyond scandal, the texture of daily economic life is generating discontent that macroeconomic aggregates do not capture. Rental costs in Athens and Thessaloniki have risen sharply, driven by short-term letting platforms and insufficient housing supply. Inflation, while retreating from its peak, has permanently repriced food and energy for households that did not benefit proportionally from the recovery. Revelations of fraud in European Union agricultural subsidy programmes have added to a sense that the gains of fiscal stabilisation are not being distributed fairly.

Trade unions and opposition voices argue that primary surpluses, while fiscally impressive, come at the cost of public investment and wage protection. The government counters that it has raised the minimum wage and introduced targeted social transfers, arguing that critics offer no coherent economic alternative. The debate reflects a genuine disagreement about who bears the burden of fiscal discipline.

Strong Growth Figures Offer a Contested Source of Optimism

The macroeconomic picture, viewed through official data, is considerably more positive. The European Commission projects GDP growth of 2.1 percent for 2025 and 2.2 percent for 2026, comfortably above the eurozone average, according to its spring forecasts. Greece has run a primary surplus of approximately 4.5 percent of GDP, exceeding initial targets, while unemployment is expected to fall to 8.6 percent, still among the higher rates in the EU but a marked improvement from crisis-era peaks. Public debt is forecast to fall below 140 percent of GDP by 2027, a trajectory that would have seemed implausible a decade ago.

The OECD has confirmed that Greece has outpaced the broader eurozone in growth terms consistently since 2021, crediting structural reforms and the mobilisation of EU Recovery and Resilience Facility funds for investments in energy, digital infrastructure, and green transition projects. Mitsotakis has used international forums, including dialogue with French business and government interlocutors, to position Greece as a southern European investment destination, emphasising its improved sovereign credit ratings and stable fiscal framework.

However, the OECD has also flagged persistent structural weaknesses that temper the optimism. Tax evasion remains a significant drain on public revenues, reducing the fiscal space available for social spending. Labour shortages in key sectors, particularly hospitality, agriculture, and construction, point to skill mismatches that require sustained investment in workforce training. Perhaps most significantly, analysts and government officials alike acknowledge that growth driven substantially by RRF disbursements faces a natural ceiling as those funds are absorbed, making private investment attraction the central economic challenge of the coming years.
articles.keywords: Greece, Mitsotakis, Nea Dimokratia, Greek politics, Greek economy, eurozone growth, fiscal surplus, PASOK, SYRIZA, Tempi disaster, Predator spyware, EU Recovery and Resilience Facility, political fragmentation, coalition government, cost of living, Greek debt, unemployment, OECD, European Commission
articles.sources: European Commission Spring 2025 Economic Forecasts; OECD Economic Surveys, Greece; Eurostat Fiscal Data; Greek opinion polling aggregates, 2024–2025; Magda Karystianou civic movement polling data; Tempi rail disaster parliamentary inquiry records

articles.title: Italy's Growth Trap: Stagnant Economy and Soaring Debt Put Rome Under Pressure
articles.excerpt: Italy is caught in a deepening economic bind, with GDP growth stuck at just 0.5% in 2025 and public debt on course to peak near 138% of GDP within two years. Despite the Meloni government's claims of fiscal credibility and credit rating upgrades, independent forecasters and opposition economists warn that austerity without investment is tightening a vicious circle. The combination of demographic pressure, weak domestic demand, and geopolitical headwinds is leaving Italian households with stagnant incomes and diminishing confidence in the state's capacity to deliver.
articles.body:
A Recovery That Never Quite Arrived

Italy's economy grew by just 0.5% in 2025, well below the eurozone average, cementing a trajectory that has seen annual expansion fall from 0.9% in 2023. For the third-largest economy in the euro area, the persistence of near-zero growth is not merely a statistical disappointment — it is a structural warning sign. Net exports subtracted approximately 0.7 percentage points from GDP in 2025, while domestic demand contributed a modest one percentage point, leaving overall output barely moving.

Forecasters aligned with Eurostat project that Italy's growth will remain in a narrow band of 0.4% to 0.8% through 2027, offering little prospect of the acceleration needed to stabilise public finances or lift living standards. The OECD has urged Rome to protect investment flows under the National Recovery and Resilience Plan, warning that the alternative — cutting productive spending to meet fiscal targets — risks undermining the very foundations of any future rebound.

The human dimension of this stagnation is stark. Real GDP per capita in Italy has risen by less than 10% since the mid-1990s, compared with gains of 30% to 40% in comparable European peers over the same period. Citizens, according to analysts tracking consumer behaviour, have responded by hoarding savings amid uncertainty rather than spending, further suppressing the domestic demand the economy urgently needs.

Debt Dynamics and the Limits of Fiscal Prudence

The government of Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni has sought to frame Italy's fiscal management as a story of hard-won credibility. Officials point to credit rating upgrades received in 2025, a deficit reduction path targeting 2.6% of GDP by 2027, and continued deployment of recovery funds toward green and digital transition projects. The narrative is of a serious, market-trusted administration navigating difficult terrain with discipline.

Independent data tells a more complicated story. Italy's public debt stood at between 136% and 137% of GDP in 2025, and EU projections indicate it will peak at approximately 137.9% in 2026 before edging down only marginally to 137.2% by 2027. Critics, including economists such as Veronica De Romanis, argue that primary budget surpluses are insufficient to overcome the arithmetic of interest-growth differentials — that is, the gap between what Italy pays on its debt and the rate at which its economy expands. When that gap is negative, debt tends to rise regardless of fiscal effort.

The 2025 deficit came in at approximately 3.1% of GDP, above the EU's 3% threshold and a figure that risks triggering closer scrutiny from Brussels. Analysts note that spending pressures from an aging population — which is simultaneously shrinking the workforce, straining pension budgets, and reducing future tax receipts — are structural in nature and cannot be resolved through short-term consolidation alone. High debt levels also constrain Italy's ability to respond to future shocks, whether economic or geopolitical, leaving limited fiscal space in a world that has produced the war in Ukraine and continued instability across the Middle East.

What Comes Next: Reforms, Rules, and a Restless Public

The coming two years will test whether Rome can reconcile the competing demands of EU fiscal rules, demographic pressures, and the investment requirements of a modernising economy. The NRRP represents Italy's most significant lever for structural transformation, channelling European funds into infrastructure, digitalisation, and the green transition. However, the pace of disbursement and the quality of project implementation remain subjects of ongoing concern among EU officials and domestic analysts alike.

The opposition and a growing number of independent economists argue that fiscal consolidation, pursued too aggressively or without accompanying growth measures, risks entrenching the very stagnation it aims to escape. Investment in education and innovation — areas where Italy has chronically underperformed relative to northern European peers — has, according to critics, been squeezed rather than protected in recent budget cycles. Without those investments, the productivity gains required for sustainable debt reduction remain out of reach.

Public sentiment reflects a resignation rather than outright revolt — a population making incremental adjustments to reduced expectations rather than demanding abrupt political change. Yet the underlying erosion of confidence in institutions' ability to deliver upward mobility and long-term returns, particularly for younger generations facing both labour market precarity and demographic burden, represents a slow-burning political risk. Ahead of the next Italian budget cycle and amid continuing EU fiscal surveillance, the Meloni government will need to show not just that it can balance the books, but that balancing them is compatible with growth. So far, the evidence that it can do both remains elusive.
articles.keywords: Italy economy, GDP growth, public debt, fiscal consolidation, Meloni government, eurozone, NRRP, deficit, demographic pressure, European Union, austerity, economic stagnation
articles.sources: Eurostat economic forecasts for Italy, 2025; OECD Economic Surveys, Italy; EU Commission Excessive Deficit Procedure monitoring data; National Bank of Italy macroeconomic assessments; Veronica De Romanis, public commentary on Italian fiscal policy; Italian Ministry of Economy and Finance, NRRP progress reporting

articles.title: Portugal at a Crossroads: Democracy's Anniversary and Energy Fragility Test a Divided Nation
articles.excerpt: Portugal marked the 52nd anniversary of the Carnation Revolution on April 25, 2026, a celebration amplified by the 50th anniversary of the country's first democratic elections, even as deep social grievances and memories of last year's Iberian blackout cast a shadow over the festivities. The commemorations arrived against a backdrop of parliamentary paralysis, a housing crisis, and unresolved questions about the country's energy infrastructure. For many Portuguese, the gap between the promises of 1974 and the realities of 2026 has never felt wider.
articles.body:
Carnations and Contradictions: A Democracy That Feels Unfinished

Lisbon's streets filled once again with red carnations on April 25, the flowers that became the symbol of the bloodless military coup that ended nearly five decades of authoritarian rule under the Estado Novo regime. This year's Freedom Day carried exceptional symbolic weight: 2026 marks both the 52nd anniversary of the revolution itself and the 50th anniversary of Portugal's first free democratic elections, held in April 1976. Parades, open-air concerts, and civic demonstrations drew tens of thousands across Lisbon, Porto, and smaller cities throughout the country.

Prime Minister's office communications emphasized national unity, framing the anniversary as evidence of Portugal's durable democratic institutions and its successful integration into the European Union. Officials pointed to civil liberties, macroeconomic stabilization, and youth civic engagement programs as proof that the revolution's core promises had been honoured. The government of the Democratic Alliance, which holds a fragile minority of 91 seats in parliament, has sought to use the occasion to project stability, positioning itself against what it described as "populist threats," a reference widely understood to mean the continued rise of the hard-right Chega party.

Yet that message of continuity met vocal resistance. Trade unions and opposition movements used the anniversary to highlight what they described as the revolution's unfulfilled social contract. They pointed to a deepening housing crisis, persistent wage stagnation, and labor precariousness as modern echoes of the inequalities the 1974 coup was meant to dismantle. The December 2025 general strike, which according to organizers drew participation from roughly 60 percent of the active workforce — an estimated three million people — remained a fresh reference point for critics arguing that the Democratic Alliance's minority government lacks both the mandate and the capacity to address structural problems.

One Year After the Blackout: Infrastructure Anxiety Lingers

Alongside the political symbolism of Freedom Day, April 2026 also brought reflection on a different kind of national trauma. The Iberian blackout of April 2025, which plunged Portugal and Spain into darkness and disrupted transport, hospitals, commerce, and daily life for hours, marked its one-year anniversary in the final days of the month. For many Portuguese, the event remains a benchmark of institutional failure and a source of persistent unease about the country's critical infrastructure.

Government representatives cited the absence of a repeat incident as evidence that corrective measures had taken effect. Officials pointed to grid upgrade investments, European Union co-funded renewable energy projects, and enhanced coordination with Spanish grid operators as indicators that the system was more resilient than it had been twelve months prior. Portugal has been one of the more aggressive adopters of renewable energy generation within the EU, and government sources argued the blackout had accelerated an already ongoing modernization process rather than exposing a structural flaw.

Opposition voices and labor representatives pushed back sharply on that framing. They argued the blackout had revealed chronic underinvestment in public infrastructure that a single year of reactive spending could not remedy. Union officials linked the energy crisis to the same grievances that had animated the December 2025 strike, contending that the broader pattern of underfunded public services represented a systemic failure of governance rather than an isolated technical event. Some on the left renewed calls for greater state control over energy distribution networks, an idea that the government has consistently rejected as incompatible with EU single market obligations.

Parliamentary Gridlock Clouds the Path Forward

Both debates — over democratic social promises and over energy resilience — ultimately converge on the same structural problem: Portugal's parliament is deeply fragmented, and the minority Democratic Alliance government has limited room to maneuver. With only 91 seats, Prime Minister Luis Montenegro's administration depends on tacit support or abstention from other parties to pass legislation, a dynamic that has slowed budgetary and reform processes throughout the current term. Analysts noted that the anniversary period, while emotionally powerful, was unlikely to produce legislative momentum on the contested issues of housing affordability, wage policy, or infrastructure investment.

The rise of Chega complicates the picture further. The hard-right party has made significant electoral gains by channeling frustration with mainstream politics, and its rhetoric around immigration, public spending, and national identity has reshaped the terms of political debate. The governing Democratic Alliance has sought to contain Chega's influence partly through symbolism — invoking the antifascist legacy of April 25 — while simultaneously refusing formal coalition arrangements with the party. Critics from the left argued this approach was both politically convenient and strategically incoherent.

Looking ahead, the next stress test for Portugal's minority government will likely come in the form of budgetary negotiations later in 2026, where the competing demands of EU fiscal frameworks, domestic social spending pressures, and infrastructure investment will need to be reconciled without a stable parliamentary majority. Whether the emotional resonance of the Carnation Revolution's anniversaries translates into a renewed political consensus on social priorities remains the central open question. For now, the carnations have been placed, the speeches delivered, and the country moves forward — aware of how far it has come, and uncertain about the road ahead.
articles.keywords: Portugal, Carnation Revolution, Freedom Day, April 25, democratic anniversary, Iberian blackout, energy infrastructure, Democratic Alliance, Chega, Luis Montenegro, parliamentary gridlock, housing crisis, labor strike, Lisbon, EU energy policy
articles.sources: Euronews coverage of Iberian blackout one-year anniversary, April 2026; Portuguese government official communications, April 25, 2026; Trade union statements on December 2025 general strike; Portuguese parliament composition data, Democratic Alliance minority government records; Historical records on Carnation Revolution (April 25, 1974) and first democratic elections (April 1976)

articles.title: Spain's Economic Strength Masks a Government Paralysed by Budget Gridlock
articles.excerpt: Spain's economy is outpacing most of its European peers, with GDP growth forecast at 2.9 percent for 2025 and unemployment falling to its lowest level in years. Yet behind those headline figures lies a government that has not passed a budget since 2023, operating on prorogued spending plans while coalition tensions and geopolitical shocks push any new fiscal framework beyond March 2026. The disconnect between economic resilience and political dysfunction is becoming one of Europe's most striking contradictions.
articles.body:
A Government Running on Borrowed Time and Borrowed Budgets

Spain entered 2025 without a functioning budget of its own design. Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez's coalition government has been operating under rolled-over spending plans from previous years, unable to secure the parliamentary majority needed to pass new fiscal legislation. The most recent failure came in November 2025, when the government's proposed spending ceiling was rejected in parliament, effectively resetting any timeline for a comprehensive budget to well beyond the first quarter of 2026.

Officials in Madrid have pointed to extraordinary external circumstances to explain the deadlock. The conflict involving Iran and the subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz sent natural gas prices surging by more than 60 percent, according to government statements, prompting ministers to argue that any credible budget could only be drafted once new macroeconomic forecasts were available. Economy Minister Carlos Cuerpo said updated projections were expected "in coming months," a phrase that has become a recurring feature of the government's communications since 2023.

The opposition has rejected that framing outright. The centre-right Partido Popular, alongside Vox and parts of the left including Podemos, argue that the delays reflect chronic parliamentary weakness rather than prudent crisis management. Sánchez governs with a minority coalition that depends on shifting alliances with Catalan, Basque, and other regional parties whose support cannot be taken for granted on fiscal matters. World Bank governance data places Spain's political stability index at zero, a figure that critics say speaks louder than any ministerial statement.

Strong Growth Data Offers the Government Partial Cover

The economic picture, viewed in isolation, would appear to vindicate at least part of the government's argument. European Commission and International Monetary Fund forecasts project Spanish GDP growth at 2.9 percent for 2025, comfortably above the eurozone average. Unemployment, long one of Spain's most damaging structural weaknesses, has fallen to around 10.4 percent, driven by sustained domestic demand, rising wages, and an expanding labour force bolstered by migration. Inflation, meanwhile, is projected to ease toward 2.0 percent by 2026, bringing relief to households that suffered acutely during the post-pandemic price surge.

European Union recovery funds under the Next Generation EU programme have also played a measurable role, channelling investment into infrastructure, green transition projects, and digital modernisation. The government has cited this pipeline as evidence that fiscal strategy is being pursued through alternative mechanisms even in the absence of a formal annual budget. Debt, though elevated, is forecast by the Commission to fall below 100 percent of GDP after 2026 as consolidation efforts take hold and revenue from strong economic activity increases.

Opposition parties counter that the growth figures obscure fragilities that could unravel quickly. Public debt remains above 100 percent of GDP in the near term, a burden that was worsened by emergency spending following the devastating floods that struck parts of Valencia and surrounding regions. Spain's unemployment rate, while falling, remains among the highest in the European Union, and critics argue that buoyant growth driven by tourism and consumption is not a substitute for the structural reforms that a properly debated and enacted budget would enable.

The Risk of Paralysis in an Election Horizon

The practical consequences of Spain's budget impasse are accumulating. Public institutions face uncertainty over multi-year investment commitments. Regional governments, many of which depend on transfers from Madrid, struggle to plan beyond short fiscal cycles. Businesses operating in sectors sensitive to government procurement or regulatory change cite planning difficulties as a growing concern. European Commission officials have noted in country-specific recommendations that fiscal framework credibility is essential for sustaining Spain's growth trajectory into the latter half of the decade.

The political timeline adds a further layer of pressure. National elections are due no later than 2027, and both major parties are already positioning for what is expected to be a tightly contested campaign. Sánchez's government has an incentive to avoid the electoral liability of a prolonged austerity debate, while the opposition calculates that continued gridlock strengthens its argument that the current coalition is structurally incapable of governing. Public protests over energy bills and the cost of living have added an emotional charge to what might otherwise be a technical fiscal debate, deepening distrust of political institutions across the spectrum.

The most likely scenario, according to analysts tracking Madrid closely, is that Spain will remain on prorogued budgets through at least mid-2026, with any new fiscal framework contingent on either a political breakthrough in parliament or a shift in the coalition arithmetic following potential regional or European political developments. If the March 2026 deadline passes without agreement, pressure on Sánchez to call early elections may intensify from within his own parliamentary allies. For now, Spain presents Europe with a paradox it has not fully resolved: a country growing faster than most of its neighbours, governed by institutions that cannot agree on how to spend the proceeds.
articles.keywords: Spain, budget crisis, Pedro Sánchez, fiscal policy, GDP growth, political stability, coalition government, eurozone, EU economy, public debt, unemployment, Next Generation EU, Iran war, energy prices, Partido Popular
articles.sources: European Commission Economic Forecast, Spring 2025; International Monetary Fund World Economic Outlook, 2025; World Bank Worldwide Governance Indicators; Eurostat National Accounts Data; Spanish Ministry of Economy public statements; European Central Bank inflation projections

articles.title: Greece Navigates a Paradox of Growth and Discontent as Mitsotakis Faces Eroding Mandate
articles.excerpt: Greece's economy is outpacing much of the eurozone, yet the government of Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis is losing political ground as voters grow weary of scandals, rising rents, and uneven recovery. With Nea Dimokratia's polling lead shrinking significantly and the opposition fragmented, the country faces a period of political uncertainty that could complicate its economic trajectory. The tension between creditable macroeconomic data and street-level discontent is now defining Greek politics.
articles.body:
A Shrinking Lead in a Fragmented Landscape

Nea Dimokratia, the ruling centre-right party, remains the dominant force in Greek politics but is no longer the commanding presence it once was. Opinion surveys now place the party at around 25 to 31 percent support, down from roughly 40 percent at its peak, a decline that reflects voter fatigue after years in power and the cumulative weight of several damaging controversies.

The opposition, rather than coalescing into a credible alternative, has splintered across ideological lines. The centre-left PASOK trails below 15 percent, the once-dominant SYRIZA has collapsed to between 5 and 7 percent, while Hellenic Solution, a nationalist party, has settled at 10 to 12 percent. A further 7 to 10 percent of voters are distributed among smaller movements, including a new civic initiative linked to Magda Karystianou, a campaigner who emerged from the aftermath of the 2023 Tempi rail disaster and whose movement draws around 31.8 percent sympathy among those seeking systemic change, according to recent polling data.

This fragmentation raises a practical and structural concern: under Greece's electoral system, no single party currently appears positioned to govern alone, making coalition arithmetic increasingly complex. Analysts warn that a hung parliament would test institutions still rebuilding their credibility following the economic crisis of the previous decade.

Scandals and the Cost of Living Deepen Public Frustration

The political headwinds facing Mitsotakis are not merely the product of time in office. Two episodes in particular have embedded themselves in public consciousness. The Predator spyware affair, in which Greek journalists and politicians were reportedly surveilled using commercial surveillance software, raised serious rule-of-law questions that opposition parties have repeatedly pressed. The Tempi rail crash of February 2023, which killed 57 people and exposed systemic failures in railway management and state oversight, continues to define perceptions of governmental accountability.

Beyond scandal, the texture of daily economic life is generating discontent that macroeconomic aggregates do not capture. Rental costs in Athens and Thessaloniki have risen sharply, driven by short-term letting platforms and insufficient housing supply. Inflation, while retreating from its peak, has permanently repriced food and energy for households that did not benefit proportionally from the recovery. Revelations of fraud in European Union agricultural subsidy programmes have added to a sense that the gains of fiscal stabilisation are not being distributed fairly.

Trade unions and opposition voices argue that primary surpluses, while fiscally impressive, come at the cost of public investment and wage protection. The government counters that it has raised the minimum wage and introduced targeted social transfers, arguing that critics offer no coherent economic alternative. The debate reflects a genuine disagreement about who bears the burden of fiscal discipline.

Strong Growth Figures Offer a Contested Source of Optimism

The macroeconomic picture, viewed through official data, is considerably more positive. The European Commission projects GDP growth of 2.1 percent for 2025 and 2.2 percent for 2026, comfortably above the eurozone average, according to its spring forecasts. Greece has run a primary surplus of approximately 4.5 percent of GDP, exceeding initial targets, while unemployment is expected to fall to 8.6 percent, still among the higher rates in the EU but a marked improvement from crisis-era peaks. Public debt is forecast to fall below 140 percent of GDP by 2027, a trajectory that would have seemed implausible a decade ago.

The OECD has confirmed that Greece has outpaced the broader eurozone in growth terms consistently since 2021, crediting structural reforms and the mobilisation of EU Recovery and Resilience Facility funds for investments in energy, digital infrastructure, and green transition projects. Mitsotakis has used international forums, including dialogue with French business and government interlocutors, to position Greece as a southern European investment destination, emphasising its improved sovereign credit ratings and stable fiscal framework.

However, the OECD has also flagged persistent structural weaknesses that temper the optimism. Tax evasion remains a significant drain on public revenues, reducing the fiscal space available for social spending. Labour shortages in key sectors, particularly hospitality, agriculture, and construction, point to skill mismatches that require sustained investment in workforce training. Perhaps most significantly, analysts and government officials alike acknowledge that growth driven substantially by RRF disbursements faces a natural ceiling as those funds are absorbed, making private investment attraction the central economic challenge of the coming years.
articles.keywords: Greece, Mitsotakis, Nea Dimokratia, Greek politics, Greek economy, eurozone growth, fiscal surplus, PASOK, SYRIZA, Tempi disaster, Predator spyware, EU Recovery and Resilience Facility, political fragmentation, coalition government, cost of living, Greek debt, unemployment, OECD, European Commission
articles.sources: European Commission Spring 2025 Economic Forecasts; OECD Economic Surveys, Greece; Eurostat Fiscal Data; Greek opinion polling aggregates, 2024–2025; Magda Karystianou civic movement polling data; Tempi rail disaster parliamentary inquiry records

articles.title: Italy's Growth Trap: Stagnant Economy and Soaring Debt Put Rome Under Pressure
articles.excerpt: Italy is caught in a deepening economic bind, with GDP growth stuck at just 0.5% in 2025 and public debt on course to peak near 138% of GDP within two years. Despite the Meloni government's claims of fiscal credibility and credit rating upgrades, independent forecasters and opposition economists warn that austerity without investment is tightening a vicious circle. The combination of demographic pressure, weak domestic demand, and geopolitical headwinds is leaving Italian households with stagnant incomes and diminishing confidence in the state's capacity to deliver.
articles.body:
A Recovery That Never Quite Arrived

Italy's economy grew by just 0.5% in 2025, well below the eurozone average, cementing a trajectory that has seen annual expansion fall from 0.9% in 2023. For the third-largest economy in the euro area, the persistence of near-zero growth is not merely a statistical disappointment — it is a structural warning sign. Net exports subtracted approximately 0.7 percentage points from GDP in 2025, while domestic demand contributed a modest one percentage point, leaving overall output barely moving.

Forecasters aligned with Eurostat project that Italy's growth will remain in a narrow band of 0.4% to 0.8% through 2027, offering little prospect of the acceleration needed to stabilise public finances or lift living standards. The OECD has urged Rome to protect investment flows under the National Recovery and Resilience Plan, warning that the alternative — cutting productive spending to meet fiscal targets — risks undermining the very foundations of any future rebound.

The human dimension of this stagnation is stark. Real GDP per capita in Italy has risen by less than 10% since the mid-1990s, compared with gains of 30% to 40% in comparable European peers over the same period. Citizens, according to analysts tracking consumer behaviour, have responded by hoarding savings amid uncertainty rather than spending, further suppressing the domestic demand the economy urgently needs.

Debt Dynamics and the Limits of Fiscal Prudence

The government of Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni has sought to frame Italy's fiscal management as a story of hard-won credibility. Officials point to credit rating upgrades received in 2025, a deficit reduction path targeting 2.6% of GDP by 2027, and continued deployment of recovery funds toward green and digital transition projects. The narrative is of a serious, market-trusted administration navigating difficult terrain with discipline.

Independent data tells a more complicated story. Italy's public debt stood at between 136% and 137% of GDP in 2025, and EU projections indicate it will peak at approximately 137.9% in 2026 before edging down only marginally to 137.2% by 2027. Critics, including economists such as Veronica De Romanis, argue that primary budget surpluses are insufficient to overcome the arithmetic of interest-growth differentials — that is, the gap between what Italy pays on its debt and the rate at which its economy expands. When that gap is negative, debt tends to rise regardless of fiscal effort.

The 2025 deficit came in at approximately 3.1% of GDP, above the EU's 3% threshold and a figure that risks triggering closer scrutiny from Brussels. Analysts note that spending pressures from an aging population — which is simultaneously shrinking the workforce, straining pension budgets, and reducing future tax receipts — are structural in nature and cannot be resolved through short-term consolidation alone. High debt levels also constrain Italy's ability to respond to future shocks, whether economic or geopolitical, leaving limited fiscal space in a world that has produced the war in Ukraine and continued instability across the Middle East.

What Comes Next: Reforms, Rules, and a Restless Public

The coming two years will test whether Rome can reconcile the competing demands of EU fiscal rules, demographic pressures, and the investment requirements of a modernising economy. The NRRP represents Italy's most significant lever for structural transformation, channelling European funds into infrastructure, digitalisation, and the green transition. However, the pace of disbursement and the quality of project implementation remain subjects of ongoing concern among EU officials and domestic analysts alike.

The opposition and a growing number of independent economists argue that fiscal consolidation, pursued too aggressively or without accompanying growth measures, risks entrenching the very stagnation it aims to escape. Investment in education and innovation — areas where Italy has chronically underperformed relative to northern European peers — has, according to critics, been squeezed rather than protected in recent budget cycles. Without those investments, the productivity gains required for sustainable debt reduction remain out of reach.

Public sentiment reflects a resignation rather than outright revolt — a population making incremental adjustments to reduced expectations rather than demanding abrupt political change. Yet the underlying erosion of confidence in institutions' ability to deliver upward mobility and long-term returns, particularly for younger generations facing both labour market precarity and demographic burden, represents a slow-burning political risk. Ahead of the next Italian budget cycle and amid continuing EU fiscal surveillance, the Meloni government will need to show not just that it can balance the books, but that balancing them is compatible with growth. So far, the evidence that it can do both remains elusive.
articles.keywords: Italy economy, GDP growth, public debt, fiscal consolidation, Meloni government, eurozone, NRRP, deficit, demographic pressure, European Union, austerity, economic stagnation
articles.sources: Eurostat economic forecasts for Italy, 2025; OECD Economic Surveys, Italy; EU Commission Excessive Deficit Procedure monitoring data; National Bank of Italy macroeconomic assessments; Veronica De Romanis, public commentary on Italian fiscal policy; Italian Ministry of Economy and Finance, NRRP progress reporting

articles.title: Portugal at a Crossroads: Democracy's Anniversary and Energy Fragility Test a Divided Nation
