The government has leveraged that resilience to justify successive minimum wage increases, arguing they reward workers in a constrained market and stimulate domestic demand. Officials project GDP growth of 2.4 percent for 2026, framing wage acceleration as a signal of economic confidence rather than a liability.

The data, however, tells a more complicated story. Services inflation reached 3.5 percent year-on-year in January 2026, nearly double the 2.1 percent recorded for goods. That divergence alarms fiscal conservatives and opposition economists, who warn of second-round inflationary effects if wage growth in labor-intensive service sectors continues to outpace productivity gains. Food prices, while still elevated at 4.2 percent in January, have shown some moderation from earlier peaks, offering limited comfort.

Producer price inflation has decelerated more convincingly, falling to 0.9 percent year-on-year in March 2026 from 1.5 percent in February. That trajectory suggests wages have not yet ignited broad-based cost pressures across the economy. Yet services inflation remains persistently high, and opposition voices argue that an export-dependent economy cannot afford sustained cost increases without commensurate productivity improvements to offset them.

EU Funds Prop Up Construction While Manufacturing Deteriorates

Construction activity rebounded in February 2026, boosted by EU Recovery and Resilience Facility financing and the ongoing reconstruction effort following the severe floods of 2023. The government presents this as evidence that targeted public investment generates tangible multiplier effects, preserving social cohesion while the broader economy recalibrates. RRF support is framed in Ljubljana as bridging capital during a cyclical downturn, not a structural crutch.

Manufacturing tells a starkly different story. Output in the sector contracted by 1.5 percent in 2025, with metal products and transport equipment recording the sharpest declines. Those are not peripheral industries. They are the core of Slovenia's export earnings and among the most exposed to competitive pressure from lower-cost Central and Eastern European neighbours as well as from shifting global supply chains. A construction rebound, critics note, does not generate export revenue in the same way a functioning manufacturing base does.

Producer prices remain near post-pandemic elevated levels, with the index at 126.76 points in February 2026 compared to an all-time high of 128.68 in February 2023. Long-term forecasts project a gradual rise to 130.55 points by end-2026 and 133.03 by 2027, suggesting mild but persistent upward pressure rather than the deflation that would accompany genuine productivity-driven efficiency gains. Opposition economists argue this pattern is consistent with stagnation, not recovery, and that EU transfers are supporting employment in lower-productivity construction while higher-productivity export manufacturing quietly hollows out.

A Country Treading Water as Political Patience Wears Thin

The mood among Slovenians reflects what analysts describe as cautious resignation rather than acute crisis. Those working in construction and sectors directly touched by EU investment programmes report a degree of recovery. Manufacturing communities in the country's industrial heartland have experienced something closer to stagnation. That geographic and sectoral split is fuelling political fatigue, reflected in a decline in Slovenia's political stability index to 0.72 points in 2024. Coalition fragility and the perception that difficult structural choices are being deferred contribute to public weariness even in the absence of an acute shock.

Everyday cost pressures reinforce that anxiety. Despite official narratives emphasising disinflation, services inflation at 3.5 percent and food prices at 4.2 percent mean households continue to feel squeezed. The labor market's headline strength also masks quality concerns: analysts point out that as manufacturing sheds workers and service sectors absorb them, employees may be moving into lower-productivity roles rather than advancing up the value chain. A 3.5 percent unemployment rate looks robust in aggregate but obscures that underlying shift.

The outlook for Slovenia in 2026 and beyond hinges on whether EU funds and wage growth together generate the conditions for industrial modernisation, or whether they simply defer consolidation in struggling export sectors. Ljubljana faces a deadline implicit in the RRF structure: recovery financing is finite, and the construction rebound it has enabled will not last indefinitely. If manufacturing productivity does not improve, and if services inflation continues to outrun goods prices, the country risks drifting toward mid-income stagnation dressed in the language of resilience. The next round of fiscal consolidation decisions, alongside any move to expand R&D investment and skills retraining, will be the clearest signal of which path the government has chosen.
articles.keywords: Slovenia, wage growth, inflation, EU recovery funds, manufacturing contraction, services inflation, RRF, labor market, producer prices, economic stability, fiscal consolidation, GDP growth
articles.sources: Trading Economics, Slovenia Producer Prices Index, February–March 2026; Bank of Slovenia and IMAD Economic Forecasts, January 2026; Slovenia Statistical Office, Consumer Price and Manufacturing Output Data, 2025–2026; EU Recovery and Resilience Facility Progress Reports, Slovenia; World Bank Political Stability Index, Slovenia, 2024

articles.title: Bulgaria at a Crossroads: Radev's Landmark Victory and the Road to the Euro
articles.excerpt: Rumen Radev's Progressive Bulgaria coalition has secured the country's first parliamentary majority since 1997, ending years of political paralysis across eight elections in five years. The landslide win coincides with Bulgaria's imminent accession to the eurozone on January 1, 2026, a milestone overshadowed by mass youth protests and deepening public anxiety over inflation and living costs. Together, the two developments define a pivotal moment for the European Union's poorest member state.
articles.body:
A Majority That Ends Years of Gridlock

In a result that surprised even seasoned observers of Bulgarian politics, Radev's Progressive Bulgaria coalition captured between 44 and 44.7 percent of the vote in the country's most recent parliamentary election, translating into approximately 130 seats in the 240-seat National Assembly. That absolute majority, the first secured by any Bulgarian political force since 1997, ends a prolonged cycle of institutional dysfunction that had produced eight elections in five years and left essential governance tasks — including budget approvals and EU fund disbursements — effectively frozen.

Radev framed his victory in moral terms, describing it as a triumph of "hope over distrust" and pledging to pursue anti-corruption reform against entrenched oligarchic networks. His coalition drew support from a broad electoral coalition: reform-minded urban voters and economically vulnerable citizens who had grown weary of the cycle of fragmentation. The European Commission, represented by President Ursula von der Leyen, offered a measured welcome, and analysts noted that Radev would likely seek external legitimacy through continued engagement with EU institutions.

For Brussels, the practical stakes are significant. Bloomberg reported that substantial EU structural funds have remained inaccessible to Bulgaria precisely because of prior governmental instability. A functioning majority with a clear mandate could unblock that pipeline, potentially channelling billions of euros into infrastructure, digital transition, and social programmes.

The Kremlin Question and the Limits of Pro-EU Assurances

The political celebration has not been universal. Opposition parties and a number of European analysts have raised pointed concerns about Radev's track record on Russia policy. His documented opposition to EU sanctions on Moscow and his resistance to providing military aid to Ukraine have led critics to describe him as a potential "Trojan horse for the Kremlin," a figure who could erode European consensus on the war from within while maintaining a surface-level pro-EU posture. The comparison to Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán has been made explicitly and repeatedly.

A careful review of the evidence, however, suggests the picture is more complicated. Radev has consistently reaffirmed Bulgaria's commitment to both the European Union and NATO, and there is no concrete evidence of an imminent strategic realignment. His rhetoric tends toward what his supporters characterise as pragmatic nationalism — a preference for dialogue over confrontation — rather than outright Euroscepticism. Past pro-Russia positions are documented, but analysts caution against conflating sympathetic language with formal policy shifts, particularly given the institutional constraints Bulgaria faces as an EU and NATO member.

The opposition's concern is nonetheless partially credible. Radev's past statements have at times diverged from the mainstream European position at sensitive moments, and his strong majority now gives him the legislative tools to act on those instincts should he choose to do so. European partners will be watching closely, particularly on votes in the European Council related to Ukraine and Russian sanctions.

Euro Adoption, Youth Protests, and the Politics of Economic Anxiety

Overlapping with the election drama is Bulgaria's scheduled adoption of the euro on January 1, 2026. The accession process is legally irreversible under the EU treaty framework, and the government has argued that membership in the single currency will unlock more than 20 billion euros in EU cohesion funds while providing macroeconomic stability and lower borrowing costs. Supporters point to the experience of Croatia, which joined the eurozone in 2023 without the catastrophic inflation surge that critics had predicted.

Yet the path to accession has been politically turbulent. More than 100,000 demonstrators, many of them young Bulgarians, took to the streets in protest, forcing the government to withdraw a controversial budget proposal that included tax increases and higher social contributions. The protests reflected a broader sense of elite disconnection: demonstrators argued that the costs of economic adjustment were being disproportionately placed on ordinary households already struggling with high energy prices and stagnant wages. Several protest leaders called for government resignations and a fundamental rethink of fiscal priorities.

A closer examination of the protests reveals an important nuance. The demonstrations were primarily directed at the outgoing GERB administration's budget choices rather than at euro adoption itself. Eurostat data does not support claims of a unique inflationary surge tied to eurozone entry in comparable economies. Still, the public mood captures a genuine vulnerability: Bulgaria remains the EU's poorest member by GDP per capita, and any perception that monetary integration is accelerating price rises will test the political durability of Radev's new government. His anti-corruption mandate and the goodwill of his electoral majority may prove to be assets in managing that tension — but only if the government delivers visible improvements in public services and institutional accountability before the conversion date arrives.
articles.keywords: Bulgaria, Rumen Radev, Progressive Bulgaria, Bulgarian election, eurozone accession, euro 2026, Bulgaria NATO, Bulgaria EU, Bulgaria Russia, Gen Z protests, Bulgarian budget, Sofia protests, Eastern Europe, EU enlargement, Bulgarian politics
articles.sources: Reuters, Bulgarian Central Election Commission results reporting; Bloomberg, analysis of EU fund disbursement and political stability in Bulgaria; Eurostat, inflation data for eurozone accession economies including Croatia; European Commission statement on Bulgarian election results; EU Treaty framework on eurozone accession and irreversibility provisions

articles.title: Greece Leads Euro Area Growth but Political Tensions Cloud Economic Success Story
articles.excerpt: Greece's economy expanded 0.8% quarter-on-quarter in the final three months of 2025, more than doubling the euro area's 0.3% growth rate and marking the country's lowest unemployment since 2008. Yet rising inflation, eroding institutional trust, and a narrowing poll lead for the ruling New Democracy party suggest that translating macroeconomic gains into broad political support remains an unfinished task. The divergence between headline figures and household sentiment is shaping a complex debate over whether Greece's recovery is as durable as its government claims.
articles.body:
A Recovery That Outpaces Its Neighbours

Greece's gross domestic product grew 0.8% in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared with the previous three months, according to Eurostat data, outstripping the euro area average of 0.3% and reinforcing a narrative of sustained post-pandemic recovery. For the full year 2025, growth is projected at approximately 2.1%, broadly in line with forecasts from the OECD and the European Commission, driven by private consumption rising 2.4% year-on-year and fixed investment expanding 12.8% year-on-year.

The government of Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis and his centre-right New Democracy party has been quick to claim credit. Officials pointed to the implementation of European Union Recovery and Resilience Facility funds, structural reforms in the energy, labour, and digitalisation sectors, and a revival in tourism as the principal engines behind the expansion. Perhaps most strikingly, the primary budget surplus reached 12.69 billion euros against a target of 7.70 billion euros, according to data from ELSTAT and the economic research institute IOBE, a figure the government argued demonstrated fiscal discipline rare among southern eurozone members. Unemployment, meanwhile, fell to its lowest level since 2008, a milestone that carries particular weight in a country where joblessness once exceeded 27%.

EU officials in Brussels have watched developments in Athens with close interest. Greece is increasingly cited as a test case for the RRF's capacity to accelerate structural transformation in the eurozone periphery, and its fiscal performance is adding pressure to ongoing debates about whether cohesion fund rules and debt sustainability thresholds need revisiting as southern member states demonstrate renewed credibility.

Opposition Sees Fragility Behind the Figures

Critics, including the main opposition Syriza party and several trade union confederations, argue that the headline numbers obscure a more uneven reality. They point to a 9% year-on-year contraction in inventory investment and persistent reliance on imports as evidence that net exports are acting as a structural drag, raising questions about whether domestic demand can sustain itself once EU fund disbursements taper off. The opposition has also argued that wage gains remain concentrated in specific sectors and do not reflect the experience of lower-income households still grappling with elevated energy and food costs.

Inflation sits at 2.7% as of the latest available data, with the European Commission forecasting 2.8% for full-year 2025, driven by a combination of strong domestic demand and energy price dynamics. While this remains broadly in line with the eurozone average, which eased to around 2.1%, critics argue the comparison offers little comfort to households whose purchasing power has been squeezed over several consecutive years. Trade unions have called for expanded social spending and have used cost-of-living pressures as a rallying point for demands that the government face greater scrutiny over what they describe as a justice system scandal that has dented public trust in state institutions.

A fact-check of the competing claims suggests the government's position is better supported by the available data. Eurostat, OECD, and European Commission projections all corroborate the broad thrust of the recovery narrative, and the primary surplus overshoot is not in dispute. The opposition's concerns about structural fragility are not without foundation, particularly regarding import dependence and investment volatility, but the scale of those risks appears to have been overstated relative to the evidence currently available.

Political Ground Shifts as Costs Bite

The economic debate is playing out against a shifting political backdrop. New Democracy's support has slipped to between 25% and 30% in recent polls, a narrowing of the lead that the governing party enjoyed following its decisive re-election in 2023. Government advisers characterised the trend as a reflection of a fragmented opposition rather than a verdict on policy, noting that no single rival party has emerged as a credible alternative and that the polling gap, while reduced, remains intact.

That argument has some statistical grounding. The Greek opposition remains divided among Syriza, the centre-left PASOK, the Communist Party, and several smaller formations, none of which appears capable in isolation of forming a governing coalition. However, political analysts said the erosion of trust, particularly around the judiciary and the handling of high-profile scandals, represents a qualitative risk that aggregate poll numbers do not fully capture. Public mood, according to multiple surveys and business sentiment indices, is best described as contradictory: optimism among businesses and in the tourism sector coexists with a resigned frustration in many households facing costs that have not fallen as fast as the official disinflation narrative implies.

Looking ahead, the government's capacity to sustain growth above the euro area average will depend heavily on the continued deployment of RRF funds, whose disbursement timelines are subject to project completion milestones, and on whether investment momentum can be maintained without reliance on one-off fiscal transfers. For average Greeks, the coming months will test whether falling unemployment and modest wage increases are sufficient to offset inflation that, at 2.7 to 2.8%, remains above the European Central Bank's target. For Brussels, Athens will remain a closely watched data point in broader arguments about fiscal rules, debt relief, and the long-term architecture of eurozone economic governance.
articles.keywords: Greece, GDP growth, euro area, New Democracy, Kyriakos Mitsotakis, Recovery and Resilience Facility, inflation, unemployment, eurozone, Greek economy, Syriza, cost of living, fiscal surplus, European Commission, OECD
articles.sources: Eurostat, Q4 2025 GDP Flash Estimate; European Commission, Spring 2025 Economic Forecast; OECD Economic Outlook, Greece Chapter; ELSTAT, Primary Budget Surplus Data 2025; IOBE, Greek Economic Outlook Report; Greek Ministry of Finance, RRF Implementation Update

articles.title: Italy's Stagnation Problem: The Gap Between Meloni's Narrative and Economic Reality
articles.excerpt: Italy's economy is projected to grow by just 0.4 to 0.5 percent in 2025, well below the eurozone average, raising questions about the government's claims of an economic turnaround. With public debt hovering near 137 percent of GDP and productivity in long-term decline, critics argue that Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's record masks deeper structural failures. Meanwhile, a drop in Italy's political stability score adds a further complication to a government that prizes its image of durability and control.
articles.body:
A Growth Gap That Numbers Cannot Disguise

Italy's economy is expected to expand by between 0.4 and 0.5 percent in 2025, according to projections from the European Commission, the IMF, and Italy's own national statistics institute ISTAT. That figure is less than a third of Spain's 2.9 percent growth rate and significantly below the eurozone average of 1.5 percent. Even a modest recovery to between 0.7 and 0.8 percent forecast for 2026 would leave Italy trailing its peers by a substantial margin.

For ordinary Italians, the consequences are tangible. Wage growth has struggled to keep pace with the inflationary pressures of recent years, and a climate of economic uncertainty has nudged households toward saving rather than spending, further dampening domestic demand. Multiple independent forecasters agree that without significant structural reform in areas such as education, labour productivity, and innovation, Italy risks what economists describe as a self-reinforcing cycle of low growth and rising debt burdens.

Public debt remains one of the most pressing concerns. At approximately 136.4 percent of GDP, Italy carries one of the heaviest debt loads in the eurozone, a figure that limits fiscal room and keeps borrowing costs sensitive to market sentiment. The government has pointed to progress in reducing its deficit, which fell below the EU's 3 percent threshold ahead of schedule, as evidence of fiscal discipline. Independent analysts acknowledge the achievement but argue it does not address the underlying growth problem.

Meloni's Scorecard: Real Gains, Contested Significance

The Meloni government has not been without genuine economic achievements, and its supporters argue these deserve fuller recognition. Unemployment has fallen to between 6.1 and 6.2 percent, a record low for modern Italy. Female employment and average salaries have both risen. Anti-tax-evasion measures have generated additional revenue, and investor confidence in Italian sovereign debt has remained relatively stable. Ministers argue that the government inherited a fragile post-pandemic economy and that stability, rather than speed, is the appropriate metric for success.

Critics, however, argue that these data points, while real, do not add up to the "economic miracle" that government messaging has at times implied. Spain, Portugal, and Greece — countries that also endured severe post-crisis adjustments — have all recorded markedly stronger growth trajectories in the same period. Opposition economists contend that austerity-oriented fiscal policy, while satisfying EU watchdogs in Brussels, has crowded out the public investment needed to lift productivity and long-run growth.

A particular point of contention is the absence of major structural reforms. EU and IMF assessments have repeatedly identified Italy's educational system, its sluggish judicial processes, and its limited investment in research and development as bottlenecks on competitiveness. The government has launched incremental measures but has not pursued the kind of broad reform agenda that independent institutions say the scale of the problem demands.

Stability Scores, Political Tensions, and the Road Ahead

Beyond economics, a separate but related concern has emerged around Italy's political stability metrics. The World Bank's political stability score for Italy fell from 0.47 in 2023 to 0.31 in 2024, a notable deterioration that analysts say reflects growing underlying tensions even as Meloni's personal polling numbers have remained robust. The government has maintained a working coalition and avoided the kind of collapse that has historically plagued Italian administrations, but the declining score suggests that formal durability and deeper societal cohesion are not the same thing.

Observers point to a series of incidents involving far-right actors, contentious security policy debates, and a palpable gap between the government's self-confident narrative and the economic anxieties felt by many citizens. Public mood surveys and qualitative assessments describe a resigned, uncertain electorate — one that has not turned sharply against Meloni but whose spending and investment behaviour reflects little of the optimism the government projects. Security tightening measures, which the government frames as a necessary response to real threats, have drawn criticism from civil society groups who argue they deepen social divisions.

Looking ahead, Italy faces a series of interconnected tests. EU fiscal surveillance will continue to monitor whether Rome can sustain deficit reductions while preserving growth-oriented spending. The European Commission's next assessment cycle will be watched closely for any signal that Italy's structural reform commitments are falling short. At home, the government will need to demonstrate that its stability record can translate into tangible improvements in living standards if it is to maintain its political advantage heading into the next electoral cycle. For now, the distance between official narrative and independent economic data remains the defining tension in Italian public life.
articles.keywords: Italy economy, Giorgia Meloni, GDP growth, eurozone, public debt, fiscal policy, political stability, World Bank, structural reform, unemployment, Italy 2025
articles.sources: European Commission economic forecasts, 2025; IMF World Economic Outlook, 2025; ISTAT national statistics, Italy, 2025; World Bank Political Stability Index, 2023–2024; EU fiscal surveillance reports; Italian government ministerial statements

articles.title: Portugal Torn Between Democratic Resilience and Far-Right Momentum as Economy Falters
articles.excerpt: Portugal's political establishment claimed a decisive presidential victory over far-right leader André Ventura in February 2026, yet Ventura's Chega party has emerged structurally stronger despite the defeat. With growth forecasts cut to 1.8 percent and energy inflation running at nearly 5.8 percent, the country faces a convergence of economic headwinds and political fragmentation that few in Lisbon feel equipped to resolve.
articles.body:
A Presidential Win That Tells Only Half the Story

When António José Seguro was elected Portugal's president in February 2026, securing more than 65 percent of the vote against Chega leader André Ventura, the result was immediately framed by the country's establishment as a firewall holding. Seguro's victory speech called for "unity and democratic cooperation," and supporters of his cross-party coalition — stretching from the far left to the mainstream right — read the outcome as a categorical rejection of Ventura's anti-establishment platform.

Yet analysts cautioning against triumphalism have a point. Ventura, despite losing, "strengthened his Chega party" through the campaign, according to observers tracking the country's shifting political geography. The presidential office in Portugal is largely ceremonial, carrying limited executive authority. Whatever mandate Seguro claims does not translate into any reduction of Chega's capacity to obstruct, delay, or reshape legislation in parliament, where the party now stands as the largest opposition force — a position it has consolidated since its founding in 2019.

The result reflects what political scientists describe as voter bifurcation: a strong anti-far-right mobilisation among centrist and centre-left voters coexisting with a durable floor of Chega support among protest voters who feel systematically ignored. Portugal has not rejected the far right so much as contained it, and containment, as recent European history shows, is an unstable equilibrium.

Economic Pressures Give the Opposition Its Sharpest Weapon

The political fragmentation unfolding in Lisbon does not exist in a vacuum. Portugal's economy is under mounting strain, and the numbers offer Ventura and other opposition voices the raw material for sustained attack on the centre-right minority government led by Prime Minister Luís Montenegro. Growth forecasts for 2026 have been revised downward to 1.8 percent, with officials citing external shocks including the broader fallout from conflict in the Middle East and the economic disruption caused by severe Atlantic storms that battered the country's coastlines and infrastructure.

The government has sought to frame the revision as a precautionary response to forces beyond its control, arguing that 1.8 percent still represents a positive growth trajectory and that storm response has demonstrated state capacity. But the headline figure obscures a more troubling distributional reality. Inflation stood at 2.7 percent in March 2026, according to available data, but energy costs rose 5.8 percent year-on-year — a gap that falls disproportionately on working families and pensioners with limited capacity to absorb rising utility bills. Household sentiment, meanwhile, has "deteriorated significantly" regarding both personal finances and the broader economic outlook, suggesting that citizens' lived experience is diverging sharply from the macro statistics presented in official communications.

Chega has moved swiftly to exploit this divergence, attacking the government's handling of flood relief and framing the economic squeeze as evidence that traditional parties have abandoned ordinary voters. The minority government's dependence on parliamentary goodwill from both the Socialists and Chega to pass legislation means that coherent fiscal policy — including any stimulus measures that might cushion household energy costs — faces structural obstacles before it even reaches a vote. The result, critics argue, is not bold governance in difficult times but managed paralysis.

Gridlock, Protest Politics, and the Road Ahead

The immediate political horizon offers little in the way of resolution. Montenegro's government remains in a structurally weak position, its survival dependent on ad hoc legislative coalitions assembled issue by issue. That arrangement may sustain the cabinet through individual votes, but it is poorly suited to the kind of sustained fiscal or structural reform that economists argue Portugal needs to reduce its vulnerability to external shocks — whether geopolitical, environmental, or both.

For the roughly 60 percent of voters who backed Seguro, the presidential result provides a measure of relief, but anxiety persists over economic headwinds and the government's capacity to act. For the approximately one-third of voters who supported Ventura in the runoff, the sense of frustration that drove them to Chega has not diminished, and may well deepen if energy bills continue rising and storm recovery is perceived as slow or inequitable. The party's institutional presence in parliament ensures it will remain a disruptive force regardless of presidential outcomes.

The broader European context adds another layer of pressure. Portugal is not alone in watching a protest party grow stronger through defeat, and Brussels will be watching whether Lisbon can demonstrate that minority government and democratic pluralism can still deliver for citizens under duress. If economic conditions worsen through the remainder of 2026 — whether through further geopolitical escalation, another severe weather event, or a eurozone slowdown — the space for Chega's narrative will expand further. The question is not whether Portugal's democratic institutions can survive the current pressure, but whether the political centre can produce enough tangible improvement in citizens' lives to slow the erosion before the next electoral test arrives.
articles.keywords: Portugal, Chega, André Ventura, António José Seguro, far-right, presidential election, Portuguese politics, economic outlook, energy inflation, minority government, Luís Montenegro, European politics, democratic fragmentation, Atlantic storms, household sentiment
articles.sources: Reuters reporting on Portuguese presidential election results, February 2026; António José Seguro victory speech, February 2026; Portuguese National Statistics Institute inflation data, March 2026; Political analysis of Chega parliamentary consolidation, 2025–2026; Portuguese government economic outlook statement, 2026

articles.title: Spain's Governing Paradox: Economic Strength Meets Political Paralysis
articles.excerpt: Spain's minority government faces a deepening crisis after Catalan separatist party Junts withdrew its parliamentary support in October 2025, leaving Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez dependent on an unstable patchwork of regional allies. The collapse of the coalition's working majority has produced a striking contradiction: a country recording its strongest economic performance in years, yet unable to pass a national budget for the third consecutive year. The standoff, rooted in unmet commitments over Catalan autonomy and amnesty, raises fundamental questions about how long governance-by-crisis can hold.
articles.body:
A Majority Lost, a Government in Limbo

When Junts per Catalunya formally withdrew its support from Sánchez's government in October 2025, the immediate consequence was arithmetical: the ruling coalition lost its working parliamentary majority, forcing it to rely on a fragile and unpredictable alliance of regional parties including EH Bildu, ERC, and the PNV. What had already been a precarious legislative arithmetic became, in practice, a near-permanent state of managed dysfunction.

Junts leader Carles Puigdemont made his party's position unambiguous. The Socialists, he said, "may hold positions of power, but will not be able to govern." The charge is grounded in specific, documented grievances. Junts argues that the PSOE repeatedly breached the terms of the 2023 Brussels Agreement, failing to fully implement the amnesty law for leaders of the 2017 independence referendum, stalling the transfer of immigration powers to Catalonia, and allowing the Swiss mediation forum established to oversee the pact to be dissolved. In a pointed demonstration of its unpredictability, Junts announced it would communicate its voting intentions in Congress "through the screen," rejecting structured negotiation and signalling it could torpedo legislation at any moment.

Government spokesperson Patxi López described the relationship with Junts as having "ups and downs," suggesting that case-by-case deal-making in Congress remained viable. That framing, critics argue, amounts to an admission of permanent crisis management rather than coherent governance. The opposition People's Party has drawn the sharpest conclusion, arguing that Sánchez lacks democratic legitimacy and should call early elections. Sánchez, whose term runs until 2027, has given no indication of doing so.

Budget Paralysis Against a Backdrop of Economic Resilience

The clearest institutional casualty of the political deadlock is Spain's public finances — or rather, the process meant to govern them. Spain missed the October 15 deadline to present its 2026 budget, extending the 2024 spending plan via decree for a second consecutive year and marking the third straight year without a formally approved budget. The legislative stalemate has left the government reliant on spending inertia rather than strategic planning, with decisions such as extending the social electricity voucher through to the end of 2026 made by decree rather than through parliamentary deliberation.

The paradox, however, is that the underlying fiscal picture is considerably better than the political noise suggests. Spain is projected to achieve its first primary fiscal surplus — the budget balance excluding debt interest payments — since 2007. Headline inflation has eased to 2.0 percent, unemployment has fallen to 9.8 percent, and GDP growth is forecast in the range of 2.3 to 2.9 percent, driven by strong domestic demand and continued disbursement of EU recovery funds under the Next Generation EU programme. Tax revenues have outperformed forecasts, enabling the primary surplus projection even in the absence of a formal budget.

Critics, including budget hawks and regional opposition parties, warn that the picture is less reassuring than it appears. Gross public debt remains above 100 percent of GDP, despite the projected decline. Three consecutive years of budget paralysis eliminate the government's ability to redirect spending toward new priorities. And the growth model, they argue, carries structural vulnerabilities: heavy dependence on tourism and consumption leaves Spain exposed to external shocks. Without a budget, there is no mechanism to shift resources toward the productive investment that could reduce those vulnerabilities over time.

Borrowed Time: The Outlook Through 2027

Spain's situation can be summarised as follows: the government is economically competent but politically imprisoned. The World Bank's political stability index captures the deterioration in hard numbers, dropping from 0.15 in 2023 to 0.00 in 2024 — a signal of governance fragility that contrasts sharply with the economy's outperformance. Both realities are simultaneously true, and the tension between them defines Spain's near-term trajectory.

The national mood reflects this duality. Among Catalan separatists and regionalists, frustration is acute. Junts secured backing from 87 percent of its membership for the decision to withdraw support from Sánchez — a figure that speaks to deep alienation rather than tactical manoeuvring. Among economic observers and centrists, the prevailing sentiment is cautious optimism laced with anxiety: growth is real, but governance feels like a borrowed-time arrangement. For many ordinary workers, the dominant emotion may be closer to resignation. Unemployment is falling and wages are holding, but the spectacle of perpetual political deadlock has reinforced a sense that Spain functions despite its government rather than because of it.

The key question for investors, institutions, and European partners is whether decree-based governance can hold without triggering a confidence shock. For now, strong fundamentals provide a buffer. But the arithmetic will not improve before the 2027 elections, and each failed budget cycle narrows the government's room for manoeuvre. If Junts continues to vote unpredictably, if bond markets begin to price in political risk more aggressively, or if a global slowdown exposes the fragility of Spain's growth model, the distinction between political paralysis and economic crisis may prove harder to maintain than it has been so far.
articles.keywords: Spain, Pedro Sánchez, Junts, Carles Puigdemont, PSOE, Catalonia, political crisis, budget paralysis, fiscal surplus, GDP growth, Spanish economy, coalition government, Catalan autonomy, amnesty law, EU recovery funds, political stability
articles.sources: PSOE government statements and spokesperson Patxi López, October 2025; Carles Puigdemont and Junts per Catalunya public declarations, October 2025; World Bank Political Stability Index, 2023–2024; Spanish fiscal projections and primary surplus data, 2025–2026; Next Generation EU fund disbursement reports; Spanish National Statistics Institute (INE) labour market and inflation data

articles.title:
Dutch Economy Defies Political Gridlock as Coalition Talks
Stall
articles.excerpt:
The Netherlands posted 1.8 percent economic growth in the
fourth quarter of 2025 despite seven months of political deadlock
following snap elections. While robust export performance and
household consumption drive near-term resilience, a caretaker
government faces mounting pressure to address structural reforms amid
declining political stability and external risks from Middle East
disruptions and potential US tariffs.
articles.body:
Strong Growth Masks Governance Vacuum

The Dutch economy expanded 1.8 percent year-on-year in the fourth quarter of 2025, capping full-year growth of 1.9 percent, according to official statistics from CBS. The performance exceeded expectations for an economy operating under caretaker administration since October elections produced a fragmented parliament unable to form a governing coalition. Exports rose 2.6 percent and public consumption increased by the same margin, offsetting a 0.5 percent quarterly decline in fixed asset investment that signals business caution about future commitments.

The resilience reflects structural strengths in the Netherlands' export-oriented model, particularly in logistics, petroleum products, machinery, and transport equipment. Household finances remain solid as wage growth outpaces inflation, while the caretaker government's 2026 budget delivers tangible support through higher income tax thresholds, increased tax credits, minimum wage adjustments, and expanded childcare subsidies. These measures provide continuity even as coalition negotiations remain stalled seven months after voters delivered a splintered mandate.

Yet the contrast between economic momentum and political paralysis grows starker. The World Bank's political stability index for the Netherlands fell to 0.43 in 2024 from 0.61 the previous year, dropping well below the 30-year average of 0.98. The decline reflects not only coalition difficulties but deeper fragmentation around immigration policy and national identity, with Geert Wilders' Freedom Party retaining significant influence despite failing to lead government formation. Major reforms on housing supply, labor market flexibility, and long-term fiscal sustainability remain frozen while party leaders negotiate behind closed doors.

External Risks Test Logistics Hub Model

The Netherlands faces asymmetric vulnerability to Middle East conflict despite appearing less exposed than neighboring Germany. As a small, trade-dependent economy where logistics efficiency underpins competitive advantage, any prolonged disruption to shipping lanes or energy markets threatens margins across multiple sectors. The fourth quarter export gains occurred during a period of relative stability; analysts warn that escalation or persistence of regional conflict could quickly erode the favorable conditions assumed in official forecasts.

Government officials and some economists argue the diversified nature of Rotterdam port operations and Schiphol airport provides resilience through alternative trade corridors. Manufacturing output remained positive in January 2026, and the country's lower concentration of energy-intensive industry compared to Germany offers some insulation from commodity price shocks. The 2026 growth projection of 1.3 percent, while more modest than 2025 performance, does not signal alarm about geopolitical disruption in official planning documents.

However, the quarterly decline in fixed asset investment suggests businesses are already hedging against uncertainty. Trade unions and supply chain experts note that converging risks from potential US tariffs, Middle East instability, and energy market volatility create conditions where the 1.3 percent forecast assumes benign scenarios that may not materialize. The projected budget deficit of 2.7 percent of GDP leaves limited fiscal space for responding to external shocks without decisive political leadership that currently does not exist.

Caretaker Limits Constrain Policy Response

The caretaker administration can manage day-to-day economic operations and implement the approved 2026 budget, but lacks authority to pursue transformative initiatives. Housing shortages, labor market rigidities, and sustainability transitions require legislative action that a fragmented parliament cannot deliver without a formal coalition agreement. Opposition parties and policy analysts increasingly question whether economic fundamentals can sustain momentum if governance paralysis extends through mid-2026 or beyond.

The political impasse reflects deeper tensions within Dutch society. Populist pressures on immigration policy complicate coalition mathematics, as parties struggle to reconcile economic pragmatism with identity politics that animated the October campaign. Even after a government eventually forms, the narrow margins and ideological divisions suggest constrained capacity for bold policymaking. The contrast with previous eras of stable coalition governance marks a structural shift in Dutch political culture that economists warn could eventually undermine investor confidence and business planning.

Near-term indicators remain favorable, with consumer spending supported by real wage gains and export sectors performing well. Yet the combination of political fragmentation, external risks, and deferred structural reforms creates vulnerability beneath surface resilience. The coming months will test whether the Netherlands can maintain economic momentum through caretaker governance, or whether the absence of decisive leadership begins extracting tangible costs. Coalition negotiators face growing pressure to resolve differences before favorable conditions erode, though no breakthrough appears imminent as parties remain divided on fundamental questions of immigration, fiscal policy, and European integration.

Netherlands economy, political gridlock, coalition talks,
GDP growth, caretaker government, exports, political stability, Middle
East risks, budget deficit, economic resilience

CBS Netherlands; ING Think Economic Research; European

Finance

articles.title:
Belgium Confronts Fiscal Emergency as Debt Nears 110 Percent
of GDP
articles.excerpt:
Belgium's government debt is projected to reach 109.9 percent
of GDP in 2026, prompting the European Commission to launch an
Excessive Deficit Procedure against the country. The new De Wever
coalition government is pursuing structural reforms to pensions, labor
markets, and taxation, but the National Bank warns that Belgium has
exhausted its fiscal buffers to absorb future economic shocks. Critics
question whether the reform agenda will restore growth or deepen
economic pain for workers already facing rising
unemployment.
articles.body:
Deficit Widens Despite Austerity Push

Belgium faces a structural fiscal crisis as government debt climbs toward 109.9 percent of GDP in 2026, accompanied by a widening deficit projected to reach negative 5.5 percent of GDP despite ongoing austerity measures. The European Commission has initiated an Excessive Deficit Procedure, placing Belgium under formal surveillance as it attempts to stabilize public finances through a combination of pension adjustments, labor market reforms, and tax restructuring.

National Bank Governor Pierre Wunsch delivered a stark assessment of the country's fiscal position, warning that "the money has run out" to absorb future economic shocks. Speaking to concerns about external risks, Wunsch noted that escalating tensions in the Middle East could push Belgium into very low growth or even a technical recession, potentially adding one to two percentage points to the deficit. His comments underscore the fragility of Belgium's fiscal position after years of elevated spending on defense and rising interest payments on accumulated debt.

The De Wever coalition government, formed after eight months of political paralysis, has positioned structural reforms as the only path to restoring fiscal credibility. The reform package includes pension system overhauls, labor market flexibility improvements, and tax cuts on labor designed to boost competitiveness and attract investment. The European Commission forecasts that these measures, combined with improving external demand, will support gradual recovery to 1.1 percent GDP growth in 2026 and 1.3 percent in 2027.

Labor Reforms Trigger Job Losses Before Promised Gains

Belgium's labor market faces a paradox at the heart of the reform debate. Strict worker protections and high labor costs have long deterred employers from hiring, with businesses citing expensive dismissal procedures and rigid employment laws as barriers to expansion. The government argues that reducing these frictions will make Belgium more attractive for investment and allow companies to adapt to global competition, particularly as the country faces pressure from US tariffs affecting its fourth-largest export market.

However, the immediate impact of labor market reforms contradicts the government's competitiveness narrative. Unemployment is projected to rise to 6.2 percent in 2026 as reforms take effect, before declining modestly to 6.1 percent in 2027. Wage growth is simultaneously easing as labor supply expands amid demographic pressures, creating a squeeze for workers facing job insecurity and stagnant incomes during a period of elevated living costs. Trade unions argue that flexibility measures amount to easier firing and wage suppression, with workers bearing the cost of reforms while promised long-term gains remain speculative.

The OECD has noted that Belgium's GDP per capita continues to lag the upper half of OECD countries due to a substantial gap in employment rates, suggesting that previous reform efforts have not successfully addressed structural labor market problems. Employers remain hesitant to hire despite existing flexibility measures, raising questions about whether the current reform package will deliver different results or simply shift economic pain onto workers without generating the promised investment and job creation.

External Risks Threaten Reform Timeline

The success of Belgium's reform strategy depends heavily on external conditions that are already showing signs of deterioration. The European Commission's growth forecasts assume improving external demand and reduced uncertainty in the external environment, yet US tariffs are negatively affecting Belgian exports and Middle East tensions threaten trade stability. National Bank Governor Wunsch has explicitly warned that Belgium lacks the fiscal resources it had in 2022 to absorb crisis shocks, leaving the country vulnerable to external disruptions that could derail the reform timeline.

Political uncertainty continues to weigh on business confidence and long-term investment decisions. The eight-month coalition formation process left deep scars in public trust, and the new government's reform agenda faces skepticism from workers, unions, and economists who question whether austerity during weak demand will stimulate growth or trigger the recession Wunsch warned about. The Commission acknowledges that defense and interest expenditure are driving deficit increases, but expects the adjustment plan to stabilize debt over the medium term if external conditions cooperate.

The outlook for Belgium hinges on a delicate balance between reform implementation and economic resilience. The government must execute pension and labor market changes while maintaining enough domestic demand to avoid recession, all while navigating trade tensions and geopolitical risks beyond its control. If external demand fails to materialize as forecast, Belgium could face a scenario where reforms increase unemployment and reduce consumption precisely when the economy needs support, potentially validating opposition concerns that the timing of structural changes will deepen rather than resolve the crisis.
articles.keywords:
Belgium, fiscal crisis, government debt, deficit, structural
reforms, labor market, unemployment, European Commission, Excessive
Deficit Procedure, De Wever coalition, pension reform,
competitiveness, GDP growth
articles.sources:
European Commission economic forecasts; National Bank of
Belgium; OECD economic surveys; Belgian government coalition
agreement

articles.title:
French Business Leaders Court Far-Right as Fiscal Pressures
Mount
articles.excerpt:
Major French business executives are increasingly engaging
with the far-right Rassemblement National ahead of the 2027
presidential election, seeking to influence the party's economic
platform as France grapples with a deficit exceeding 6 percent of GDP.
The outreach marks a significant shift in RN's status from political
pariah to potential power broker, even as the government implements
billions in spending cuts. The dual developments threaten to reshape
French economic policy and deepen political fragmentation across the
eurozone's second-largest economy.
articles.body:
Business Elite Breaks Taboo on Far-Right Engagement

Senior executives from some of France's most prominent corporations have begun meeting regularly with leaders of the Rassemblement National, signaling a dramatic evolution in the far-right party's relationship with the business community. Marine Le Pen and party president Jordan Bardella have hosted dinners and lunches with representatives from LVMH, TotalEnergies, and the influential Medef business federation, according to multiple sources familiar with the discussions. An April 7 dinner with CAC 40 executives and a subsequent Medef lunch with Bardella represent the most visible signs of this engagement.

The outreach reflects RN's efforts to soften its historically anti-business image and present itself as a credible governing alternative ahead of the 2027 presidential election. Party leaders have pledged a "simplification shock" to reduce bureaucracy, promised deregulation measures, and advocated for national procurement policies designed to favor French companies. RN officials argue these positions align with business frustrations over what they characterize as President Emmanuel Macron's regulatory failures and economic stagnation.

Yet the engagement remains fraught with contradictions. While RN has moderated some of its most extreme anti-free trade positions, core elements of its platform including euroscepticism, welfare expansions such as reversing pension reforms, and national priority clauses continue to conflict with EU treaty obligations and the interests of export-oriented sectors. Left-wing unions, particularly the CFDT, have condemned the business contacts as cynical legitimization of a party whose policies could harm working-class voters and European integration. Leaked accounts of the meetings suggest ongoing tensions, with some executives expressing concern over policy coherence despite the party's polling strength.

Deficit Breach Triggers Austerity Measures

France's fiscal position has deteriorated sharply, with the 2024 deficit reaching 6.1 percent of GDP, up from 5.5 percent the previous year and well above the European Union's 3 percent limit. The overshoot has forced Prime Minister François Lecornu to announce 6 billion euros in spending cuts for 2026, split between 4 billion euros from state budgets and 2 billion euros from social security programs. The measures form part of a broader consolidation effort estimated at between 44 billion and 50 billion euros, equivalent to 1.4 percent of GDP, planned for 2025.

Government officials have attributed the fiscal deterioration to external shocks including Middle East tensions, alongside sluggish economic growth of just 1.1 percent driven by reduced public spending. They argue the cuts are necessary to stabilize public finances, avoid intervention by the European Central Bank, and create conditions for recovery. Data from the OECD supports the government's assessment of fiscal pressures, though the organization has suggested milder adjustments tied more closely to controlling public spending growth rather than sharp reductions.

Opposition parties and labor unions have attacked the austerity program as counterproductive, arguing it will deepen economic stagnation by cutting social security and state investment at a time when private sector activity remains paralyzed by political uncertainty. A hung parliament resulting from recent elections has complicated passage of budget measures, with the government surviving multiple no-confidence votes. Both RN and left-wing parties have criticized the cuts as punishing ordinary citizens while business leaders explore alternatives with the far right, a juxtaposition that has fueled public dissatisfaction visible in opinion polling.

Political Fragmentation Clouds Economic Outlook

The convergence of business-RN engagement and fiscal crisis reflects deeper instability in French politics with implications extending beyond national borders. For French citizens, the developments present competing risks: RN's business-friendly pivot threatens welfare protections while promising deregulation that could widen inequality, even as government austerity squeezes households through social spending cuts. The political deadlock has stalled investment decisions and delayed implementation of economic reforms, contributing to the growth slowdown.

At the European level, France's persistent deficit breach raises concerns about fiscal contagion and pressure on ECB rules governing eurozone members. As the bloc's second-largest economy, France's fiscal trajectory and potential shift toward a eurosceptic government in 2027 could undermine European integration efforts and complicate coordination on economic policy. The normalization of RN as a potential governing party, facilitated in part by business engagement, represents a significant political realignment with uncertain consequences for EU cohesion.

The path forward depends heavily on the government's ability to implement its consolidation plan amid parliamentary fragmentation and on RN's capacity to reconcile its business outreach with core ideological positions. With the 2027 presidential election approaching, both the fiscal adjustment timeline and the evolution of RN's economic platform will shape France's economic trajectory and its role within the European Union. Current polling suggests public resignation to fragmentation and cuts, with RN positioned as a change agent despite unresolved questions about its governing program.
articles.keywords:
France, Rassemblement National, Marine Le Pen, Jordan
Bardella, fiscal deficit, austerity, business engagement, EU fiscal
rules, 2027 election, French economy
articles.sources:
Reuters; The Economist; IMF Fiscal Monitor; OECD Economic
Outlook; Medef; CFDT

articles.title:
Germany's Merz Confronts Seven-Decade Economic Stagnation
With Fiscal U-Turn
articles.excerpt:
Chancellor Friedrich Merz faces Germany's longest economic
stagnation in seventy years as growth flatlines at 2019 levels through
early 2025. His government has abandoned fiscal orthodoxy, proposing
deficit-financed reforms to revive manufacturing and infrastructure
amid energy shocks and geopolitical uncertainty. Critics accuse the
former fiscal hawk of hypocrisy, warning that surging debt risks
entrenching decline rather than reversing it.
articles.body:
Stagnation Grips Europe's Largest Economy

Germany's economy has stagnated for an unprecedented period, with gross domestic product remaining at 2019 levels through the first quarter of 2025. The European Commission forecasts growth of just 0.2 percent for the year, marking the longest stretch of economic inertia in seven decades. Manufacturing output has declined sharply, exports have fallen, and high energy costs continue to weigh on industrial competitiveness. The malaise has made the economy the top concern for 23 percent of German voters, according to recent polling data.

Chancellor Friedrich Merz, who took office in May 2025 following protracted coalition negotiations, has inherited an economy battered by multiple shocks. The war in Ukraine disrupted energy supplies and trade flows, while rising tensions in the Middle East have driven oil prices higher. In response, Merz's government introduced temporary fuel tax cuts of 17 cents per liter for two months to cushion households from price spikes linked to the Iran conflict. Yet these measures address symptoms rather than underlying structural weaknesses, analysts say.

The stagnation has hit export-dependent sectors hardest. Germany's automotive industry, long a pillar of prosperity, has shed jobs as demand weakens and competition from Asian manufacturers intensifies. Business sentiment remains cautious, with investment decisions delayed amid what the OECD describes as a fog of policy uncertainty. For ordinary Germans, the result is job insecurity, stagnant wages, and eroded purchasing power despite government relief efforts.

Fiscal Hawk Turns Spender

Merz has proposed what he calls radical reforms for 2026, including tax cuts, increased public spending, and large-scale investments in infrastructure and defense. The measures represent a sharp departure from his earlier stance. As opposition leader, Merz criticized what he termed debt madness and championed Germany's constitutional debt brake, which limits structural deficits to 0.35 percent of GDP. Now his government is bypassing that constraint through targeted exceptions, projecting deficits will rise to 4 percent of GDP by 2026.

The fiscal shift aims to address what Merz has called military dwarfism and infrastructure gaps exposed by the Ukraine war and trade disruptions. Defense spending is set to increase substantially, while investments in transport networks and energy systems are planned to restore competitiveness. Government officials argue the spending is essential to counter geopolitical shocks and reverse the manufacturing decline that has hollowed out Germany's industrial base.

Opposition parties have seized on the reversal. Social Democrats and Greens accuse Merz of hypocrisy, arguing he is chiseling economic decline into the constitution by enabling trillion-euro debt accumulation without addressing structural problems. Critics point to continued contraction in 2023 and 2024 as evidence that spending alone cannot revive growth. The debate has unfolded against a backdrop of political instability, with Germany's coalition stability index hitting a historic low of 0.12 in 2024 following the collapse of the previous government.

Implications for Germany and the European Union

The economic and fiscal challenges carry consequences beyond Germany's borders. As the European Union's largest economy, Germany's stagnation acts as a drag on bloc-wide growth. The European Commission has warned that prolonged weakness in Germany raises the risk of a broader eurozone recession, particularly if other member states face similar headwinds. Germany's shift toward higher deficits could also normalize looser fiscal norms across the EU, straining the Stability and Growth Pact that limits government borrowing.

For German citizens, the outlook remains uncertain. If Merz's reforms succeed in reviving investment and productivity, the economy could return to a sustainable growth path. But if deficits spiral without corresponding gains in output, taxpayers may face higher burdens in the future through spending cuts or tax increases. Energy costs remain a wild card, with exposure to Middle Eastern supply disruptions and the incomplete transition to renewable sources leaving households and businesses vulnerable to price shocks.

The government's ability to deliver on its promises will be tested in the coming months. Implementation of the 2026 reforms depends on parliamentary approval and coordination with regional governments, both of which face political headwinds. Business groups have called for clarity on tax policy and regulatory frameworks to unlock private investment. Meanwhile, the European Commission and OECD have urged Germany to pair fiscal stimulus with structural reforms to labor markets, planning rules, and energy policy. Whether Merz can navigate these pressures while maintaining coalition unity will determine whether Germany breaks free from stagnation or remains mired in decline.
articles.keywords:
Germany, Friedrich Merz, economic stagnation, debt brake,
fiscal policy, European Union, manufacturing decline, energy costs,
deficit spending, Ukraine war
articles.sources:
European Commission economic forecasts; OECD Germany economic
outlook; German government fiscal policy statements; polling data on
public concerns

articles.title:
Dutch Economy Defies Political Gridlock as Coalition Talks
Stall
articles.excerpt:
The Netherlands posted 1.8 percent economic growth in the
fourth quarter of 2025 despite seven months of political deadlock

household consumption drive near-term resilience, a caretaker

declining political stability and external risks from Middle East
disruptions and potential US tariffs.

Household finances remain solid as wage growth outpaces inflation, while the caretaker government's 2026 budget delivers tangible support through higher income tax thresholds, increased tax credits, minimum wage adjustments, and expanded childcare subsidies. GDP growth, caretaker government, exports, political stability, Middle
